title: DAG Motif Census
summary: Complete computational methodology for network motif census on directed acyclic attribution graphs. Covers igraph API specifics (motifs_randesu parameters, return format, isomorphism class numbering vs MAN label ordering), DAG-impossible motif identification (only 4 connected 3-node and 24 connected 4-node types possible in DAGs), DAG-constrained null model construction via Goni et al. four methods with topological-ordering-based acyclicity enforcement, Z-score and Significance Profile formulas with edge cases, computational feasibility for 50-500 node graphs, 8-item pitfalls checklist, software tools comparison, and a 9-step implementation recipe.
answer:
## Computational Methodology for Network Motif Census on Directed Acyclic Graphs

### 1. igraph motifs_randesu() — Complete API Specification

The primary function for motif census in igraph is `Graph.motifs_randesu(size=3, cut_prob=None, callback=None)`, which implements the ESU (Enumerate Subgraphs) algorithm originally described by Wernicke & Rasche in the FANMOD tool [15]. For directed graphs, the `size` parameter supports values of 3 (returning 16 isomorphism classes, IDs 0–15) or 4 (returning 218 isomorphism classes, IDs 0–217) [1, 2]. The return value is a list where each index corresponds to an isomorphism class ID, with the value being the count of that motif type, or `NaN` for non-weakly-connected classes [1]. The number of directed graph isomorphism classes — 16 for 3-node and 218 for 4-node — is confirmed by OEIS sequence A000273 [10].

The `cut_prob` parameter is a list of floats (length equal to `size`) controlling probabilistic branch pruning in the ESU search tree [1, 15]. Setting all values to 0.0 (or passing `None`) yields exact enumeration; setting values like `[0, 0, 0.5, 0.8]` for 4-node motifs prunes the search tree probabilistically, trading accuracy for speed [15]. The optional `callback` parameter accepts a function called for each motif instance found, receiving vertex IDs and the isomorphism class [2].

For 3-node directed graphs, 13 of the 16 classes are weakly connected and return integer counts, while 3 return NaN (the disconnected classes) [1, 13]. For 4-node directed graphs, 199 of 218 are weakly connected [13].

**CRITICAL: motifs_randesu() vs triad_census() ordering.** The order of triads is NOT the same for `triad_census()` and `motifs_randesu()` [1]. The `triad_census()` function returns results in the standard Davis-Leinhardt MAN label order (003, 012, 102, 021D, 021U, 021C, 111D, 111U, 030T, 030C, 201, 120D, 120U, 120C, 210, 300), while `motifs_randesu()` uses igraph's internal isomorphism class ID ordering [1, 2]. Internally, `triad_census()` calls `motifs_randesu()` and remaps via an internal permutation array [1]. The MAN naming convention, where M=Mutual, A=Asymmetric, N=Null counts the dyad types, with trailing letters D(own), U(p), C(yclic), T(ransitive) distinguishing subtypes, was defined by Davis & Leinhardt (1972) [1].

The **only reliable method** to build the isoclass-ID-to-MAN-label mapping is programmatic: use `Graph.Isoclass(n=3, cls=i, directed=True)` for each ID 0–15, inspect the edge structure of each canonical graph, and match to MAN labels [2]. Community discussions on the igraph forum confirm this approach as the standard solution [3]. For 4-node graphs, this same approach scales to 218 classes using `Graph.Isoclass(n=4, cls=i, directed=True)` [2].

The complete 3-node MAN label table (in triad_census order) is: index 0 = 003 (empty graph, 0 edges), index 1 = 012 (single edge A→B), index 2 = 102 (mutual A↔B), index 3 = 021D (out-star B→A, B→C), index 4 = 021U (in-star A→B, C→B), index 5 = 021C (chain A→B→C), index 6 = 111D (A↔B←C), index 7 = 111U (A↔B→C), index 8 = 030T (feed-forward A→B→C, A→C), index 9 = 030C (3-cycle A→B→C→A), index 10 = 201 (A↔B↔C), index 11 = 120D, index 12 = 120U, index 13 = 120C, index 14 = 210, index 15 = 300 (complete graph) [1].

### 2. DAG-Impossible Motifs — Validation Constraint

A directed acyclic graph (DAG) has two structural constraints: NO mutual (bidirectional) edges (which form 2-cycles) and NO directed cycles of any length [11]. These constraints eliminate specific triad types from the motif census.

**DAG-impossible due to mutual edges (M > 0):** 9 triads — 102, 111D, 111U, 201, 120D, 120U, 120C, 210, 300. All contain at least one mutual (bidirectional) edge, which constitutes a 2-cycle forbidden in DAGs [11].

**DAG-impossible due to directed 3-cycle:** 1 triad — 030C. This contains the cycle A→B→C→A despite having no mutual edges [11].

**DAG-possible but disconnected (NaN in output):** 2 triads — 003 (empty graph) and 012 (single edge). These are not weakly connected, so motifs_randesu returns NaN [1].

**DAG-possible AND weakly connected:** Only **4 types** — 021D (out-star: A←B→C), 021U (in-star: A→B←C), 021C (directed chain: A→B→C), and 030T (feed-forward loop: A→B→C, A→C) [11, 12]. This is confirmed by OEIS sequence A101228, which gives exactly 4 weakly connected unlabeled DAGs on 3 nodes [12].

This is a **critical finding**: the 3-node motif spectrum for a DAG is only **4-dimensional**. This dramatically limits discriminative power for clustering tasks. The 030T feed-forward loop is the only 3-edge connected triad possible in a DAG, making it particularly important — Mangan & Alon (2003) showed it is the most significant motif in transcription networks [21], and recent work confirms FFLs are the most abundant triangular motif in regulatory networks [22].

**For 4-node motifs:** Of 218 total isomorphism classes [10], only 31 are acyclic (OEIS A003087) [11], and of those, **24 are weakly connected** (OEIS A101228) [12]. The effective 4-node motif spectrum dimensionality is **24**, substantially richer than the 4D 3-node spectrum. Of the 199 weakly-connected 4-node directed classes [13], 175 are DAG-impossible. Building a complete catalog of the 24 DAG-possible 4-node classes requires programmatic enumeration via `Graph.Isoclass(n=4, cls=i, directed=True)` followed by filtering for acyclicity [2].

**Validation rule:** After computing motifs_randesu on a real attribution graph, ALL DAG-impossible motif IDs must have count = 0. If any are non-zero, the graph is not a true DAG — this serves as a critical data integrity check [11]. For 3-node: 10 of 16 types must be zero. For 4-node: 187 of 218 types must be zero.

### 3. DAG-Constrained Null Model Construction

The standard Maslov-Sneppen rewiring algorithm, widely used in tools like mfinder and FANMOD for generating null models of general directed graphs, does NOT preserve acyclicity — it swaps edges without checking for cycles [4, 5, 19]. The original algorithm repeatedly selects two random edges (u,v) and (w,x) and swaps to (u,x) and (w,v), preserving the degree sequence but potentially creating cycles [19]. For DAG null models, every proposed swap must be validated against acyclicity [4].

The foundational work on DAG-constrained randomization is by Goñi, Corominas-Murtra, Solé & Rodríguez-Caso (2010), published in Physical Review E [4, 5]. They define **four randomization methods** based on which topological invariants are preserved:

**Method 1 (DD — Directed Degree preserving):** Preserves the directed degree sequence (in-degree and out-degree of each node), graph size, and density. The swap operation picks two edges A→B and C→D uniformly at random, proposes swapping to A→D and C→B, and REJECTS if: (a) the new edge already exists (multi-edge), or (b) the swap creates a directed cycle [4, 5]. This is the **recommended primary null model** because it preserves the most natural structural constraint while maximally randomizing the wiring pattern [4].

**Method 2 (DD+C):** Preserves directed degree sequence AND connected component structure [4, 5].

**Method 3 (UD — Undirected Degree preserving):** Preserves undirected degree sequence (total degree regardless of direction) [4, 5].

**Method 4 (UD+C):** Preserves undirected degree sequence AND component structure [4, 5].

**Acyclicity enforcement** uses topological ordering: pre-compute a topological ordering via a leaf-removal algorithm. An edge u→v is valid only if topo_order(u) < topo_order(v). After proposing a swap, check if the new edges respect the topological order — if they do, the swap is guaranteed cycle-free. If not, perform a local DFS/BFS cycle check in the affected region [4, 5]. For efficient incremental cycle detection, Bernstein et al. (2018) provide an algorithm achieving Õ(m√n) total update time [20]. An alternative simpler approach is to attempt a full topological sort after each swap at O(V+E) cost per swap [4].

**Mixing parameters:** Goñi et al. recommend 10×|E| to 100×|E| attempted swaps, using up to 50,000 swaps for their test networks [4, 5]. The ending criterion is a dissimilarity parameter D > 0.95, measuring how much the randomized graph differs from the original [5]. The acceptance rate is typically 40–80% (20–60% rejection rate due to cycle creation or multi-edge avoidance), with denser graphs having higher rejection rates [4, 5].

**Pseudocode for Method 1 (DD):** (1) Input DAG G=(V,E). (2) Compute topological ordering T. (3) Set swap_attempts = 100×|E|. (4) For each attempt: pick two edges (u1,v1) and (u2,v2) at random; skip if u1==u2 or v1==v2; propose (u1,v2) and (u2,v1); reject if multi-edge; check if T[u1]<T[v2] and T[u2]<T[v1] (topological order preserved); if not, run cycle check and reject if cycle found; if accepted, perform the swap and update T if needed. (5) Return randomized DAG [4, 5].

**Alternative approaches:** (a) **Configuration model for DAGs** — generate random DAGs with the same degree sequence from scratch by fixing a topological ordering, assigning stubs according to the degree sequence, and connecting stubs respecting the ordering. This avoids mixing-time issues of Markov chain approaches [4]. (b) **Layer-constrained randomization** — for LLM attribution graphs where nodes have layer assignments, only swap edges between nodes that respect the layer ordering, preserving the layered structure as an additional domain-specific constraint [4].

### 4. Z-Score Computation and Significance Profile Normalization

The Z-score for motif type i was defined by Milo et al. (2002) [6] and is computed as:

**Z_i = (N_real_i − mean(N_rand_i)) / std(N_rand_i)**

where N_real_i is the count of motif type i in the real graph, mean(N_rand_i) is the mean count across all randomized graphs, and std(N_rand_i) is the standard deviation [6, 16]. A motif is usually regarded as statistically significant if |Z_i| > 2 (corresponding to P < 0.01 under Gaussian assumption) [6] [8]. The MAVISTO tool documentation provides a clear reference implementation of this formula [16]. Z_i > 0 means the motif is over-represented relative to random; Z_i < 0 means under-represented [6].

The **Significance Profile (SP)** was introduced by Milo et al. (2004) [7] to enable comparison across networks of different sizes:

**SP_i = Z_i / sqrt(sum_j(Z_j^2))**

This normalizes the Z-score vector to unit length (Euclidean norm = 1) [7]. Two networks with the same relative pattern of motif over/under-representation will have similar SPs even if their absolute Z-scores differ by orders of magnitude [7]. The SP enabled discovery of "superfamilies" — groups of networks from completely different domains (transcription networks, food webs, electronic circuits, social networks) that share similar local structural properties [7].

**Number of random graphs:** A minimum of 100 randomized graphs is needed, but 1000 is recommended for stable standard deviation estimates [6, 8]. With 1000 random graphs × 250 real graphs, this means 250,000 motif census computations [8].

**Edge cases requiring special handling:**

(a) **Zero standard deviation, both zero:** If std(N_rand_i) = 0 AND N_real_i equals the mean (typically both are 0), set Z_i = 0. The motif is absent in both real and random graphs [6, 16].

(b) **Zero standard deviation, nonzero real count:** If std(N_rand_i) = 0 AND N_real_i > mean(N_rand_i), this indicates extreme significance. Options include capping Z_i at a large value like 10, reporting as "infinite significance," or using empirical p-values instead [6, 9, 16]. Recommended: cap at Z = 10 and flag for manual review.

(c) **All Z-scores zero:** SP is undefined (0/0 division). This means the graph's motif profile is indistinguishable from random — flag as "random-like" and exclude from SP-based clustering [7].

(d) **Non-Gaussian distribution problem:** The Z-score implicitly assumes Gaussian-distributed motif counts in random graphs. Megchelenbrink et al. (2020) demonstrated this assumption often fails, showing that Z-scores can produce p-values off by hundreds of thousands of orders of magnitude [9]. Motif count distributions may be Poisson, binomial, multimodal, or zero-inflated, especially for small or sparse graphs [8, 9]. The recommended solution is to always compute BOTH Z-scores (for SP normalization and cross-network comparison) AND empirical p-values: p_i = (count of random graphs where N_rand_i >= N_real_i) / (total random graphs) [9, 16]. Picard et al. (2008) proposed a compound Poisson approximation as another alternative that avoids the Gaussian assumption [9].

**DAG-specific dimensionality:** For DAGs, the 3-node SP is a **4-dimensional** unit vector (only 021D, 021U, 021C, 030T have non-zero counts) [12]. The 4-node SP is up to a **24-dimensional** unit vector [12].

### 5. Computational Complexity and Feasibility Analysis

**3-node motif census:** The naive approach examines all C(n,3) triples = O(n^3) [8]. For n=500, this is approximately 20.8 million triples. With igraph's C backend implementing the ESU algorithm (which is edge-based and faster for sparse graphs), exact enumeration completes in well under 1 second for sparse graphs [8, 15]. **Always use exact enumeration** with `cut_prob=None` or `[0,0,0]` for 3-node motifs [15]. Total cost for a full pipeline: 1000 null models × 250 graphs × <1 second ≈ 250,000 seconds ≈ 69 hours on a single core, but this is embarrassingly parallel [8].

**4-node motif census:** Naive complexity is O(n^4) [8]. For n=500, C(500,4) ≈ 2.6 billion — potentially slow. However, igraph's ESU exploits edge-based enumeration, making it much faster for sparse graphs [15]. For sparse DAGs (average degree 5–10), expect 1–10 seconds per graph; for denser graphs (average degree 20+), may take minutes [8, 15]. The sampling option `cut_prob=[0, 0, 0.5, 0.8]` reduces time by approximately 10× but introduces sampling variance [15]. Most algorithms practically find motifs ≤8 nodes in large networks; size-10 becomes intractable [8].

**Recommended strategy by graph size:** For graphs under 200 nodes: exact 4-node enumeration. For 200–500 nodes: exact 3-node plus sampled 4-node with cut_prob tuning (or exact 4-node if edge density is low). For over 500 nodes: sample both, though attribution graphs are typically 50–500 nodes [8, 15].

**Null model generation cost:** The edge swap procedure requires 100×|E| attempted swaps per null model [4, 19]. For |E|=1000, that is 100,000 swaps. Each swap involves O(V+E) acyclicity check in the worst case, or O(1) amortized with maintained topological ordering [4, 20]. Total: approximately 0.1–1 second per null model. For 1000 null models × 250 graphs = 250,000 null models at ~0.5 seconds each ≈ 125,000 seconds ≈ 35 hours on a single core [4]. This is embarrassingly parallel: ~2 hours on 16 cores, ~30 minutes on 64 cores [4, 8].

**Total pipeline estimate:** 3-node only: ~4–8 hours on 16 cores (null model generation dominates). 3-node and 4-node combined: ~8–24 hours on 16 cores depending on graph density. Recommendation: run 3-node first as a fast validation, then add 4-node if the 4D spectrum proves insufficient for clustering [8].

### 6. Critical Pitfalls and Edge Cases Checklist

**Pitfall 1 — Weighted vs. unweighted:** igraph's motifs_randesu operates on UNWEIGHTED graph topology, but attribution graphs have weighted edges [1, 17]. Two approaches: (a) binarize by threshold — include edge if weight > threshold, running with multiple thresholds (50th, 75th, 90th percentile) as a robustness check; (b) run census on unweighted topology and separately analyze weight distributions within motif instances [17, 18]. Binarization can cause significant information loss [17], and weighted analysis can even reverse significance profiles [18]. Recommended: use approach (a) with 3+ thresholds; if results are threshold-sensitive, this is itself an informative finding.

**Pitfall 2 — Multi-edges and self-loops:** Attribution graphs may contain self-loops or parallel edges, but igraph's motif functions assume simple graphs [1, 17]. Solution: simplify before census via `g.simplify(multiple=True, loops=True, combine_edges='max')`, using 'max' to keep the strongest connection or 'sum' to aggregate. Validate with `assert g.is_simple()` [17].

**Pitfall 3 — Disconnected components (NaN handling):** motifs_randesu returns NaN for non-weakly-connected isomorphism classes — these are expected, not errors [1]. Filter NaN entries when computing Z-scores. For DAGs, expect NaN for classes corresponding to 003 and 012 (3-node) plus additional disconnected 4-node classes [1].

**Pitfall 4 — Non-Gaussian Z-score assumption:** As documented by Megchelenbrink et al. (2020), the Gaussian assumption for motif count distributions often fails, especially for small or sparse graphs [9]. Extremely high Z-scores (e.g., >2000) reported in some studies correspond to impossibly small p-values under the Gaussian assumption, indicating the assumption cannot hold [9]. Always compute empirical p-values alongside Z-scores [9, 16]. Use empirical p-values for significance testing; use SP (from Z-scores) for cross-network comparison [7, 9].

**Pitfall 5 — motifs_randesu vs triad_census ordering:** These use DIFFERENT orderings of the 16 isomorphism classes [1]. Code that maps motif IDs to MAN labels by assuming index position will produce WRONG results. Build the explicit mapping programmatically using `Graph.Isoclass(n=3, cls=i, directed=True)` [2, 3].

**Pitfall 6 — DAG validation check:** After computing motifs on each attribution graph, verify that `graph.is_dag() == True` and that all DAG-impossible motif IDs have count = 0 [11]. For 3-node: 10 of 16 types must be zero. For 4-node: 187 of 218 types must be zero. Non-zero DAG-impossible counts indicate residual bidirectional edges or cycles [11].

**Pitfall 7 — Layer-induced structure:** Attribution graphs have natural layer ordering (LLM layers) that constrains motif possibilities beyond basic acyclicity [4]. Standard null models may not respect this. Recommended: run BOTH layer-agnostic (standard DD method) and layer-preserving (only swap edges respecting layer distances) null models. If results differ substantially, layer structure is a significant contributor [4, 5].

**Pitfall 8 — Small graph instability:** For graphs with fewer than 30 nodes, motif counts are very low (single digits), making Z-scores unreliable [8, 9]. Recommended minimum thresholds: ≥50 nodes for 3-node census, ≥100 nodes for 4-node census. For smaller graphs, report raw counts without Z-score normalization, or aggregate across multiple small graphs [8].

### 7. Software Tools Comparison

**igraph (Python)** — PRIMARY recommendation. Provides `motifs_randesu()` for 3-node and 4-node directed graphs, `Graph.Isoclass()` for motif identification, and `triad_census()` for quick 3-node analysis [1, 2]. C backend ensures high performance. Limitations: only supports size 3–4 for directed graphs; no built-in DAG null model generation [1]. Install: `pip install igraph` [2].

**graph-tool** — SECONDARY recommendation for performance-critical 4-node analysis. Provides `motifs(g, k)` supporting arbitrary k with OpenMP parallel support and C++ backend [14]. Returns actual Graph objects for each motif class. Limitations: more complex installation, different API conventions [14]. Install: `conda install -c conda-forge graph-tool` [14].

**NetworkX** — NOT RECOMMENDED for motif analysis. Only provides `triadic_census()` for 3-node; no general motif census for 4-node. Pure Python implementation is very slow [8]. Use only for quick prototyping or verification.

**FANMOD** — ALTERNATIVE for standalone analysis. Very fast C++ implementation with built-in sampling (RAND-ESU) and null model generation [15, 19]. Limitations: command-line only, harder to integrate into Python pipelines, no DAG-specific null models [15].

**Kavosh/NetMODE** — SPECIALIZED academic tools. Kavosh can find larger motifs than FANMOD and uses NAUTY for isomorphism testing [8, 19]. Limitations: harder to install, less maintained [8].

### 8. Step-by-Step Implementation Recipe

**Step 1 — Prepare input graph:** Load attribution graph. Remove self-loops and multi-edges via `g.simplify()` [17]. Verify `g.is_dag() == True` [11]. Binarize weighted edges using threshold (e.g., 75th percentile) [17, 18].

**Step 2 — Build isoclass mapping table:** For i in 0..15: construct `Graph.Isoclass(n=3, cls=i, directed=True)`, inspect edge structure, assign MAN label [2, 3]. Mark each as DAG-possible or DAG-impossible [11]. Store as dictionary. Repeat for 4-node (0..217) if doing 4-node analysis [2].

**Step 3 — Compute real graph motif census:** Run `g.motifs_randesu(size=3, cut_prob=None)` for exact 3-node counts [1]. Optionally run size=4 (exact or sampled) [15]. Replace NaN with 0 for processing. Verify DAG-impossible types have count = 0 (validation check) [11].

**Step 4 — Generate null model ensemble:** For each real graph, generate 1000 randomized DAGs using Goñi Method 1 (DD) [4, 5]. For each: copy original graph, perform 100×|E| swap attempts with acyclicity check, verify the randomized graph is still a DAG, compute motif census [4].

**Step 5 — Compute Z-scores:** For each motif type i: Z_i = (N_real_i - mean(N_rand_i)) / std(N_rand_i) [6, 16]. Handle edge cases: if std=0 and N_real=0, set Z=0; if std=0 and N_real>0, cap Z=10 [6].

**Step 6 — Compute empirical p-values:** For each motif type i: p_i = count(N_rand_i >= N_real_i) / 1000 [9, 16]. Report alongside Z-scores as a guard against non-Gaussian distributions [9].

**Step 7 — Compute Significance Profile:** SP_i = Z_i / sqrt(sum(Z_j^2)) [7]. If all Z=0, flag as 'random-like' and exclude from clustering. The SP is a 4D unit vector (3-node) or up to 24D (4-node) [7, 12].

**Step 8 — Build motif spectrum matrix:** Stack SP vectors for all attribution graphs into a matrix (N_graphs × D_motif_types). This is the input for downstream clustering [7].

**Step 9 — Robustness checks:** Repeat Steps 1–8 with different edge weight thresholds [17, 18]. Compare SP vectors across thresholds. If clustering is stable across thresholds, results are robust [18].

### Key Numbers Summary

| Metric | 3-node | 4-node |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Total isomorphism classes [10] | 16 | 218 |
| Weakly connected classes [13] | 13 | 199 |
| DAG-possible total [11] | 6 | 31 |
| DAG-possible AND connected [12] | 4 | 24 |
| Motif spectrum dimensionality | 4 | 24 |

Recommended null models: 1000 per graph [6, 8]. Minimum graph size: 50 nodes (3-node), 100 nodes (4-node) [8, 9].

sources.title: igraph C Documentation: Graph Motifs, Dyad Census and Triad Census
sources.summary: Official igraph documentation for motifs_randesu, triad_census, and related functions. Confirms ordering difference between the two functions and NaN for disconnected classes.

sources.title: igraph Python API Reference: GraphBase
sources.summary: Python API documentation for Graph.Isoclass(), motifs_randesu(), isoclass(), triad_census(). Confirms support for size 3-4 directed graphs.

sources.title: igraph Forum: How to map motifs_randesu output to graph structures
sources.summary: Community discussion explaining Graph.Isoclass() usage for mapping motif indices to structures.

sources.title: Goni et al. (2010): Exploring the randomness of Directed Acyclic Networks
sources.summary: Foundational paper on DAG-constrained null models. Defines four randomization methods preserving different topological invariants. Uses topological ordering to enforce acyclicity during edge swaps.

sources.title: Goni et al. (2010): Full paper PDF (Phys. Rev. E 82:066115)
sources.summary: Full text with leaf-removal algorithm, swap operations, dissimilarity convergence criterion D>0.95, validation on C. elegans, PhD networks, and Milgram citation network.

sources.title: Milo et al. (2002): Network Motifs: Simple Building Blocks of Complex Networks
sources.summary: Original paper introducing network motifs. Defines Z-score formula, degree-preserving randomization, and significance criteria.

sources.title: Milo et al. (2004): Superfamilies of Evolved and Designed Networks
sources.summary: Introduces the Significance Profile (SP) normalization formula SP_i = Z_i / ||Z||. Defines network superfamilies based on SP similarity.

sources.title: Kim et al. (2012): Biological network motif detection: principles and practice
sources.summary: Comprehensive review of motif detection algorithms (ESU, RAND-ESU, Kavosh, MODA). Compares exact enumeration vs sampling, discusses Z-score and P-value computation, complexity analysis.

sources.title: Megchelenbrink et al. (2020): Intrinsic limitations in mainstream methods of identifying network motifs in biology
sources.summary: Critical analysis showing Z-scores can be misleading under non-Gaussian motif count distributions. Reports p-value discrepancies of hundreds of thousands of orders of magnitude.

sources.title: OEIS A000273: Number of unlabeled simple digraphs with n nodes
sources.summary: Sequence: 1, 3, 16, 218, 9608. Confirms 16 isomorphism classes for 3-node directed and 218 for 4-node directed.

sources.title: OEIS A003087: Number of unlabeled acyclic digraphs with n nodes
sources.summary: Sequence: 1, 2, 6, 31, 302. Gives the total number of non-isomorphic DAGs: 6 for 3-node, 31 for 4-node.

sources.title: OEIS A101228: Number of weakly connected acyclic digraphs on n unlabeled nodes
sources.summary: Sequence: 1, 1, 4, 24, 267. Gives the number of weakly connected DAG isomorphism classes: 4 for 3-node, 24 for 4-node.

sources.title: Wolfram MathWorld: Weakly Connected Digraph (OEIS A003085)
sources.summary: Sequence A003085: 1, 2, 13, 199, 9364. Confirms 13 of 16 three-node and 199 of 218 four-node directed isomorphism classes are weakly connected.

sources.title: graph-tool Documentation: motifs() function
sources.summary: Documents graph-tool's motifs(g, k, p, motif_list, return_maps) function. Supports arbitrary k, sampling via p parameter, OpenMP parallelism.

sources.title: Wernicke & Rasche (2006): FANMOD: a tool for fast network motif detection
sources.summary: Describes the RAND-ESU algorithm underlying igraph's motifs_randesu. ESU provides exact enumeration; RAND-ESU adds probabilistic branch pruning for sampling.

sources.title: MAVISTO: Z-score and P-value for Network Motifs
sources.summary: Defines Z-score as (F_observed - F_mean) / sigma. P-value as probability that randomized network frequency >= observed frequency.

sources.title: Poulin et al. (2019): Nine Quick Tips for Analyzing Network Data
sources.summary: Warns against binarizing weighted networks due to significant information loss. Recommends careful handling of self-loops and multi-edges.

sources.title: Motif-based spectral clustering of weighted directed networks (2020)
sources.summary: Discusses weighted motif analysis approaches. Shows weighted analysis can reverse significance profiles found in binary analysis.

sources.title: Patra (2020): Review of tools and algorithms for network motif discovery in biological networks
sources.summary: Comprehensive tool comparison (FANMOD, Kavosh, MODA, mfinder, NetMODE). Documents computational complexity, null model switching method with ~100*E iterations.

sources.title: Bernstein et al. (2018): Improved Algorithm for Incremental Cycle Detection
sources.summary: State-of-the-art incremental cycle detection with O-tilde(m*sqrt(n)) total time. Relevant for efficient acyclicity checking during DAG edge swaps.

sources.title: Mangan & Alon (2003): Structure and function of the feed-forward loop network motif
sources.summary: Foundational analysis of the feed-forward loop (030T) as a key network motif in transcription networks. The FFL is the only 3-edge connected triad possible in DAGs.

sources.title: Inferring links in directed complex networks through feed forward loop motifs (2023)
sources.summary: Confirms FFL is the most abundant triangular motif in transcriptional regulatory networks, greatly outnumbering cyclic triangles.

follow_up_questions: What are the exact igraph isomorphism class IDs for each of the 4 DAG-possible connected 3-node triads (021D, 021U, 021C, 030T)? This requires running Graph.Isoclass() programmatically to build the mapping.
What is the actual wall-clock time for 4-node exact motif census on a 500-node sparse DAG (average degree 5-10) using igraph's C backend? This requires empirical benchmarking.

For the 24 DAG-possible weakly-connected 4-node isomorphism classes, which specific subgraph structures do they represent? Building this catalog requires programmatic enumeration with Graph.Isoclass(n=4, cls=i, directed=True) and filtering for acyclicity.
